content:
**Must know**
• The **neuron** (nerve cell) is the structural and functional unit of the nervous system — it is **unicellular**.
• Two main parts: **cyton** (cell body) and **axon** (long extension).
• The **cyton** contains the **nucleus** and short branching **dendrites** that receive impulses from other neurons.
• The **axon** carries impulses **away** from the cyton to muscles, glands or the next neuron.
• A **myelin sheath** (in many neurons) speeds up impulse transmission along the axon.
• Neurons are abundant in the **brain** and **spinal cord**.
• Three kinds of neurons: **sensory** (receptor → CNS), **motor** (CNS → effector), **association/relay** (within the CNS).
• At a **synapse**, the impulse crosses from the axon of one neuron to the dendrites of the next — impulses flow in **one direction** only.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Impulse** — a wave of electrical disturbance that runs through nerves.
• **Synapse** — the junction between two neurons for passing impulses.
• **Dendron** — the part of a neuron that carries impulses towards the cyton.

**Exam traps:**
• Neurons are NOT multicellular — each neuron is a single cell.
• Dendrites **receive** impulses; the axon **transmits** them — do not swap the roles.
• Impulses travel **towards** the CNS through sensory neurons and **away** through motor neurons.
teacherTip: Cyton = control centre (nucleus inside). Axon = long cable carrying the message out. Draw a labelled neuron before every nervous-system answer.
examTip: For 'describe a neuron' questions, name cyton + axon, state it is unicellular, and mention that the cyton holds the nucleus.

content:
**Must know**
• The **nervous system** controls and coordinates all body activities in response to internal and external changes.
• **Central Nervous System (CNS)** = **brain** + **spinal cord** — the command centre where information is received, processed and responses decided.
• **Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)** = all **nerves** that connect the CNS to every part of the body.
• PNS has two subdivisions:
  – **Somatic nervous system** — controls **voluntary** actions (walking, writing).
  – **Autonomic nervous system** — controls **involuntary** actions (heartbeat, digestion); has **sympathetic** (fight/flight) and **parasympathetic** (rest/digest) branches that act **antagonistically** (oppositely).
• **Coordination** = interlinking of body activities and organs as per the needs of the body.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **CNS** — brain and spinal cord; processes information.
• **PNS** — cranial and spinal nerves linking CNS to receptors and effectors.

**Exam traps:**
• The spinal cord is part of the **CNS**, not the PNS — nerves emerging from it are PNS.
• Sympathetic and parasympathetic systems are **opposite** in action; fear and anger activate the **sympathetic** system, not the parasympathetic.
• Do not confuse PNS with 'peripheral' meaning 'unimportant' — it carries every signal to and from the body.
teacherTip: CNS = headquarters (brain + spinal cord). PNS = telephone wires (nerves). Autonomic = autopilot for organs you never think about.
examTip: Name all three divisions when asked about the nervous system: CNS, PNS (somatic + autonomic). One mark each in long answers.

subtopic: 3. The Brain — Cerebrum, Cerebellum and Medulla
content:
**Must know**
• The brain is protected by the **skull** and three meninges; it is the largest part of the CNS.
• **Cerebrum** — largest part; occupies the whole upper area.
  – Seat of **intelligence, consciousness, memory, thinking and will power**.
  – Controls all **voluntary** activities and interprets sensations (sight, smell, touch, etc.).
  – Outer portion = **grey matter** (cell bodies); inner = **white matter** (axons).
• **Cerebellum** — lies behind and below the cerebrum ('little brain').
  – Controls **muscular coordination, balance and posture**.
  – Needed for precise movements: cycling, typing, catching a ball.
• **Medulla oblongata** — at the base of the brain, continuous with the spinal cord.
  – Controls **involuntary** vital functions: **breathing, heartbeat, blood pressure, swallowing, coughing, sneezing, peristalsis**.
• Damage to the medulla is **life-threatening** because it controls survival functions.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Grey matter** — region rich in cell bodies (cytons).
• **White matter** — region rich in myelinated axons.

**Exam traps:**
• **Cerebrum** = thinking and voluntary control; **cerebellum** = balance — never swap them (classic assertion-reason trap).
• The cerebellum does NOT control intelligence or memory — that is the cerebrum.
• The medulla controls internal organs; the cerebrum controls voluntary muscles.
teacherTip: Cerebrum = Cerebral = thinking (biggest). Cerebellum = ballerina balance. Medulla = Must-do survival (breathing, heartbeat).
examTip: Give at least two functions for each of the three brain parts. Examiners deduct marks for vague answers like 'controls the body'.

content:
**Must know**
• **Cranial nerves** — arise from the **brain**; **12 pairs** in humans.
  – Carry impulses to and from sense organs in the **head** region.
  – Examples: **olfactory** (nose → brain), **optic** (eyes → brain), **auditory** (ears → brain).
• **Spinal nerves** — arise from the **spinal cord**; **31 pairs** in humans.
  – Spread to the **rest of the body** (trunk, limbs, internal organs).
• Both cranial and spinal nerves belong to the **PNS**.
• Three kinds of nerves: **sensory** (sense organs → CNS), **motor** (CNS → muscles/glands), **mixed** (carry both sensory and motor fibres).
• **Sympathetic nerves** belong to the autonomic system — they are NOT cranial nerves.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Cranial nerve** — a nerve that emerges directly from the brain.
• **Spinal nerve** — a nerve that emerges from the spinal cord through intervertebral foramina.

**Exam traps:**
• Remember the numbers: **12** cranial pairs, **31** spinal pairs — reversed numbers lose marks.
• Optic, olfactory and auditory nerves are **cranial**, not spinal.
• A mixed nerve is not a fourth category of neuron — it is a nerve containing both sensory and motor fibres.
teacherTip: 12 cranial = head senses (smell, sight, hearing). 31 spinal = everything below the neck. Mixed nerve = two-way street.
examTip: When differentiating cranial vs spinal nerves, always state the number of pairs AND the region they supply.

subtopic: 5. Receptors, Effectors and Types of Nerves
content:
**Must know**
• **Stimulus** — any change in the environment that produces a response in the body.
• **Receptor** — a sense organ (or specialised nerve ending) that **detects** the stimulus and sends impulses **to** the CNS via **sensory (afferent) nerves**.
  – Examples: nerve endings in skin, eyes, ears, nose, tongue.
• **Response** — the activity of the body brought about by the stimulus.
• **Effector** — a **muscle** or **gland** that carries out the response on receiving impulses from the CNS via **motor (efferent) nerves**.
• **Sensory nerve** — carries impulses from receptors to the brain/spinal cord.
• **Motor nerve** — carries impulses from the brain/spinal cord to effectors.
• Direction of impulse: receptor → sensory neuron → CNS → motor neuron → effector.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Receptor** — receives the stimulus (detects change).
• **Effector** — effects the response (acts on command).

**Exam traps:**
• Receptors send impulses **to** the CNS — they do NOT receive messages from the CNS.
• Effectors receive impulses through **motor** nerves, not sensory nerves.
• Glands (e.g. salivary gland) are effectors too — effectors are not only muscles.
teacherTip: Receptor = IN (senses danger). Effector = OUT (hand pulls away). Sensory in, motor out — never reverse the direction.
examTip: For distinguish questions, use skin receptor detecting heat vs hand muscle pulling away as your standard example pair.

subtopic: 6. Reflex Action and the Reflex Arc
content:
**Must know**
• **Reflex action** — a quick, immediate, **automatic involuntary** response to a stimulus **without the involvement of the brain** (though the brain may perceive it afterwards).
• **Reflex arc** — the shortest nerve pathway from receptor to effector that makes a reflex possible.
• **Sequence:** Stimulus → Receptor → Sensory neuron → **Spinal cord** (CNS) → Motor neuron → Effector → Response.
• Reflexes below the neck are controlled by the **spinal cord** — this **short pathway** makes them faster than voluntary actions.
• Two types of reflexes:
  – **Natural (inborn)** — present from birth: blinking, coughing, sneezing, knee-jerk, withdrawing hand from heat.
  – **Conditioned (acquired)** — learned by experience: salivation on smelling food, applying brakes while cycling, typing.
• Example: touching a hot object → hand withdraws **before** pain is consciously felt.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Reflex action** — involuntary, rapid response controlled by the spinal cord.
• **Reflex arc** — the nerve pathway of a reflex from receptor to effector.

**Exam traps:**
• Reflexes are controlled by the **spinal cord**, NOT the brain — a favourite true/false question.
• The brain is bypassed for speed; saying 'reflexes have no CNS involvement' is wrong — the spinal cord IS part of the CNS.
• Salivation on smelling food is an **acquired** reflex, not a natural one.
teacherTip: Reflex = spinal cord shortcut. Brain finds out AFTER the hand is already safe. Draw the reflex arc with arrows for full marks.
examTip: Write the reflex arc sequence in order and label all five structures. Diagrams score higher than text alone in ICSE.

content:
**Must know**
• **Voluntary actions** — performed **knowingly** and under our **will**; controlled by the **cerebrum**.
  – Examples: eating, walking, reading, writing, talking, playing cricket.
• **Involuntary actions** — occur **without conscious thought**; controlled by the **medulla oblongata** and **spinal cord** (and autonomic system for organs).
  – Examples: breathing, heartbeat, digestion, peristalsis, blinking, salivation, blood pressure regulation.
• **Reflex actions** are a special category of involuntary actions — extra fast, via the spinal cord.
• We can stop some voluntary actions (hold your breath briefly) but cannot permanently stop involuntary ones (heart keeps beating).
• The **autonomic nervous system** (sympathetic + parasympathetic) fine-tunes involuntary organ activities.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Voluntary action** — action under conscious control of the cerebrum.
• **Involuntary action** — automatic action controlled by medulla/spinal cord/autonomic system.

**Exam traps:**
• Blinking is **involuntary** (reflex), even though we can sometimes blink on purpose — classify by normal function.
• Walking is voluntary but **requires** cerebellum for balance — do not say cerebellum controls voluntary actions; it coordinates them.
• Breathing is involuntary (medulla) but can be briefly overridden voluntarily — still classify it as involuntary.
teacherTip: Voluntary = you decide (cerebrum). Involuntary = body decides (medulla/spinal cord). Give one example of each in every answer.
examTip: Compare on the basis of control centre AND give examples — ICSE distinguish questions need both columns filled.

subtopic: 8. Grey and White Matter; Spinal Cord
content:
**Must know**
• **Spinal cord** — a long cylindrical cord running through the vertebral column; connects the brain to spinal nerves.
• Controls **reflexes** below the neck and relays impulses between brain and body.
• **Grey and white matter arrangement is REVERSED** between brain and spinal cord:
  – **Cerebrum:** grey matter outside, white matter inside.
  – **Spinal cord:** grey matter inside (H-shaped), white matter outside.
• Grey matter = cell bodies (cytons); white matter = bundles of axons.
• The spinal cord is protected by the **vertebral column** (backbone) and meninges.
• Injury to the spinal cord can cause **paralysis** below the injury level because impulses cannot reach effectors.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• **Grey matter** — tissue rich in neuron cell bodies.
• **White matter** — tissue rich in myelinated axons appearing white.

**Exam traps:**
• Students often write 'brain: white outside, grey inside' — it is the **opposite** (grey outside in cerebrum).
• Saying grey matter contains axons and white matter contains cell bodies is completely reversed.
• The spinal cord processes reflexes — it is not merely a passive cable.
teacherTip: Brain = grey hat on outside. Spinal cord = grey butterfly on inside. Reversed arrangement is the #1 diagram question in this chapter.
examTip: For grey/white matter questions, draw a simple cross-section of cerebrum AND spinal cord side by side — one diagram earns two marks.
